--- Thread (31 messages, 2018-04-04 to 2018-04-05) ---
[2018-04-04 23:56] xyotsuba: If shiver was cheaper and chill 1 it would be bought more
[2018-04-04 23:56] mtanzer: if shiver cost the same as 2 gauss charges yes I'd definitely start spamming it for the chill
[2018-04-04 23:56] extratricky: right so 3R nonblocking shivers would obviously be spammed
[2018-04-04 23:56] xyotsuba: Chill 2 is not even 1.5 times as good as chill 1
[2018-04-04 23:57] extratricky: sometimes chill 2 is twice as good (e.g. plexo)
[2018-04-04 23:57] extratricky: but those are cases when 5R yeti gets spammed 😛
[2018-04-04 23:57] mtanzer: yeah how good shiver is depends heavily on what's in the set.  as it is now it's a must-buy in some sets at 5R
[2018-04-04 23:57] extratricky: yeah or centurion sets
[2018-04-04 23:58] extratricky: plexo, cent, dgrid, doomed drone, chieftain, doomed wall, etc
[2018-04-04 23:58] xyotsuba: I think its good that yeti is underpowered tho
[2018-04-04 23:58] mtanzer: there's enough that the fact that shiver is very mediocre in other sets feels ok
[2018-04-04 23:58] xyotsuba: Permanent freeze is not something that should be playable in many games
[2018-04-04 23:58] extratricky: plus shiver gets played in the lategame to make gambits harder in almost every set anyway
[2018-04-04 23:59] extratricky: because you can just sub a rhino for it
[2018-04-04 23:59] mtanzer: I wouldn't mind if it got some small buff but 4R would probably be too much
[2018-04-04 23:59] extratricky: yeah that's why cryo isn't permanent
[2018-04-04 23:59] mtanzer: and there's not some natural way to change it to something between 4R and 5R.  since it does serve an important role in a decent number of sets I think it doesn't need to be changed
[2018-04-05 00:00] xyotsuba: If freeze was assigned the same as damage in a breach (none wasted) shiver would be must buy
[2018-04-05 00:00] extratricky: in most sets shiver freeze isn't wasted lategame because of R/F
[2018-04-05 00:01] extratricky: it's pretty rare that you defend without any 2 hp things
[2018-04-05 00:02] xyotsuba: But u dont wanna freeze the rhino, u wanna kill it
[2018-04-05 00:03] extratricky: sure being able to freeze EE is better than only being able to freeze R
[2018-04-05 00:04] xyotsuba: If freeze worked like that it would be so much easier to calculate...
[2018-04-05 00:04] xyotsuba: I guess it could be an issue of needing multiple clicks for one activation?
[2018-04-05 00:06] extratricky: it's also just less interesting I think
[2018-04-05 00:07] extratricky: granularity problems are fun
[2018-04-05 00:07] .bky_1556 (bky): "it's pretty rare that you defend without any 2 hp things" -> Keeping fewer 2 hp defenders than the opponent's yetis is a valid tactic vs. 3+ yetis.
[2018-04-05 00:08] .bky_1556 (bky): The defender causes this intentionally.
[2018-04-05 00:08] extratricky: yeah but that's also why attackers usually don't get 3+ yetis right now
[2018-04-05 00:08] extratricky: unless there's another compelling reason
[2018-04-05 00:09] .bky_1556 (bky): Vs. a single yeti you can arrange on some turns to have nothing between your 1-hp granularity and your wall+ sized soak, and be certain it'll only freeze an engineer.

[2018-04-05 20:01] steel0229e (Steel): assuming there is no alternate route the opponent can go, or a faster rush that leads to an equal game without cutting too much econ
[2018-04-05 20:02] steel0229e (Steel): there exists an optimal timing for every unit in base + x, if you get it earlier than turn k (optimal) you lose to more econ, if you get it a turn later you lose to the first rush

[2018-04-05 20:14] steel0229e (Steel): Solutions to base set are not overstatements. I spent many game hours and hundreds of games testing. All top palyer agrees that p2 wins base set. I agree on base+1, it should not matter much in base+8 or more units. But you really underestimate the impact of early turns. The community does not "ignore" later turns and it has nothing to d with harder analysis. Simply this is how maths work. There is inflation in the game. Early investments are HUGE. A resophore you buy late can in reality cost you up to 8 gold worth of value. A floated gold/red is a massive hit you take. An inefficient conduit straight out loses you the game. At 2k+ games, all these mistakes get punished mercilessly.
[2018-04-05 20:15] wilson0862 (Wilson): Who even cares about solutions to base set.
[2018-04-05 20:15] jeacaveo (jeacaveo (#NeverBrowserer)): I'm not doubting solutions to base set, or. even base + 1. the game is not played like that.
[2018-04-05 20:16] steel0229e (Steel): Yes, it is very marginal. But I had to defend this statement.
[2018-04-05 20:16] steel0229e (Steel): It affects balance in less than 1%.
[2018-04-05 20:17] jeacaveo (jeacaveo (#NeverBrowserer)): I used to watch top players stream (msven, mrguy, redrame, and others), and I couldn't understand why even they were confused when they lost games just because they made assumptions at the beginning (supposed optimal plays that guarantee wins).
[2018-04-05 20:17] steel0229e (Steel): when people are confused at a loss, it is often a strategic thing that they did not anticipate
[2018-04-05 20:18] steel0229e (Steel): you can call out tactical errors much easier
[2018-04-05 20:19] steel0229e (Steel): sometimes people think they found an "optimal" opening when it was not the best line
[2018-04-05 20:19] jeacaveo (jeacaveo (#NeverBrowserer)): that's what I mean, the opening turns its just a part of the equation, I hate to think the game is just that. I'm a huge newb, so it might just be wishful thinking on my part.
[2018-04-05 20:20] steel0229e (Steel): Just like in chess, openings matter a lot. You can be dead before the game gets to late game. You need a good opening just to survive and get to the stage where your tactical decisions can slowly gain you an edge. You will also come to this concliusion over time I am quite confident that openings matter more than later decisions.
[2018-04-05 20:22] steel0229e (Steel): But the better your opponent is, the less advantage you can get in the opening. They will also pick a good opening and the game gets to the later stage. When I play casual, I win many games in a few turns and I can just autopilot things from there. Against someone like Arkanishu, the game is close until the last turn and every turn there is some kind of an important decision.
[2018-04-05 20:23] steel0229e (Steel): I do not want to overstress the importance of an opening, but even the best player can lose to someone 100 elo below them if their opening was weak.
[2018-04-05 20:24] itsotter: probably more important than in chess
[2018-04-05 20:24] itsotter: chess doesnt have exponential resource growth
[2018-04-05 20:25] steel0229e (Steel): this is a snowbally game
[2018-04-05 20:25] steel0229e (Steel): you can make a lot more mistakes in the late game it will snowball less
[2018-04-05 20:26] itsotter: for april fools next year elyot should add a blue shell to "fix snowballing"
[2018-04-05 20:27] steel0229e (Steel): to make a comeback from a bad early turn, you need 3-5 turns that are played perfectly by you and a mistake by your opponent
[2018-04-05 20:28] steel0229e (Steel): luckily everyone makes mistakes, even if those are small inefficiencies
[2018-04-05 20:31] jeacaveo (jeacaveo (#NeverBrowserer)): I'm not underplaying the importance of an opening, I just have a problem with how people talk about the game in here. Dampens my spirit. Maybe I need to be more open to the conversation and stop taking people so literally?

My big issues are words like: autowin for  p1/p2, <insert turns 3/4  build order here> cant lose in set X, etc.
[2018-04-05 20:31] steel0229e (Steel): there are very few autowins, and yes people use this term too liberally
[2018-04-05 20:34] steel0229e (Steel): I used to use this word a lot when I was around 1700-1800 elo. And in general I think low masters say it the most, because that is the elo range when people don't know how to deal with certain timings or swingy unit combos. That is where the game is the most imbalanced. At higher ranks you can blame a few units for 90% of the issues in the game, and true autowins are rare but that does not mean they don't exist.
[2018-04-05 20:36] steel0229e (Steel): it is best to give the benefit of doubt and assume there was a tricky sequence of moves that created enough complications for the  game to make it seem like coinflip, but in actual games time is a big limiting factor and one side sometimes has a much easier time
[2018-04-05 20:38] .bky_1556 (bky): Basically, you need to see the "autowin" coming by turn 2.
[2018-04-05 20:38] .bky_1556 (bky): Then you can try to undercut, counter or go over the top of it.
[2018-04-05 20:38] steel0229e (Steel): I learned a lot from Apooche and 307th, they are so good that they can turn a seemingly imbalanced set around. But sometimes even they can't do magic.
[2018-04-05 20:40] steel0229e (Steel): Yeah bky, you need to be familiar with who is favoured if you go into some kind of mirror/asymmetry. Then based on that and your past experiences, you can weigh what kind of support units the set has, and whether that makes the scenarios you learned different (and favoured for who). Then you can decide for an altered timing to avoid taking a line that has a bad probability to win.
[2018-04-05 20:40] jeacaveo (jeacaveo (#NeverBrowserer)): 'magic' sounds like we can have a set their's no way they can win (and they know it by turn 4), even if played perfectly on both sides.
[2018-04-05 20:40] steel0229e (Steel): Knowing when to break symmetry is a must is an important.
[2018-04-05 20:41] steel0229e (Steel): yes jeacaveo, that can happen
[2018-04-05 20:41] .bky_1556 (bky): OTOH, 1800-level players often leak multiple gold-value from midgame technical errors, which would be enough to make a tricky undercut not go off on time.
[2018-04-05 20:43] steel0229e (Steel): I don't want to make it sound like this is an imbalanced game, but I also don't wanna maintain an illusion that it is perfect.
[2018-04-05 20:43] jeacaveo (jeacaveo (#NeverBrowserer)): @Steel I refuse to believe that. I'll make it my purpose to make that clear or  Lunarch is gonna have to do something about it.
[2018-04-05 20:43] msven: i'd just like to point out that I made a video about how to play from a disadvantaged position, for people interested in this topic
[2018-04-05 20:43] steel0229e (Steel): In theory every set is either a forced win for one side or a draw. Sometimes it is easy to find the forcing lines, sometimes it is not.
[2018-04-05 20:45] steel0229e (Steel): I also refused to believe this for years so I completely understand you. Until this very day I think Prismata is the most balanced competitive game (maybe 2nd to Go), but I no longer have illusions.
[2018-04-05 20:45] drevoed: got a link to your channel?
[2018-04-05 20:46] msven: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=eScxdpwBLx8&index=3&list=PL9gDhaOQQIWkN79kVFwuJuNYeGjmxEOCM
Playing Your Position - Prismata Expert Strategy and Tactics Part 3
If you're interested in hanging out and asking questions while I play Prismata, check out my twitch channel: https://www.twitch.tv/msven Follow me on Twitter...
[2018-04-05 20:46] msven: Prismata's balance is great, these kinds of player favouring sets don't come up that often and are usually a result of a few specific units needing stat changes
[2018-04-05 20:49] msven: but i also acknowledge that there will always be an occasional autoloss, where I've spent like an hour or more in analyze mode and couldn't find a way to win

[2018-04-05 20:53] msven: outcome uncertainty can be improved with years of experience though
[2018-04-05 20:54] Deleted User (Deleted User): Outcome uncertainty technically isnt random. I'd say the two kinds of randomness are the set generation and going first or second, althought i suppose you could argue to call it one kind of randomness, which is "game initialization"
[2018-04-05 20:54] steel0229e (Steel): you can make better and better guesses the more experience you have
[2018-04-05 20:54] steel0229e (Steel): the random set is the 3rd type of randomness

messages.author: steel0229e
messages.content: Yeah bky, you need to be familiar with who is favoured if you go into some kind of mirror/asymmetry. Then based on that and your past experiences, you can weigh what kind of support units the set has, and whether that makes the scenarios you learned different (and favoured for who). Then you can decide for an altered timing to avoid taking a line that has a bad probability to win.

messages.author: jeacaveo
messages.content: 'magic' sounds like we can have a set their's no way they can win (and they know it by turn 4), even if played perfectly on both sides.

messages.author: steel0229e
messages.content: Knowing when to break symmetry is a must is an important.

messages.author: steel0229e
messages.content: yes jeacaveo, that can happen

messages.author: .bky_1556
messages.content: OTOH, 1800-level players often leak multiple gold-value from midgame technical errors, which would be enough to make a tricky undercut not go off on time.

messages.author: steel0229e
messages.content: I don't want to make it sound like this is an imbalanced game, but I also don't wanna maintain an illusion that it is perfect.

messages.author: jeacaveo
messages.content: @Steel I refuse to believe that. I'll make it my purpose to make that clear or  Lunarch is gonna have to do something about it.

messages.author: msven
messages.content: i'd just like to point out that I made a video about how to play from a disadvantaged position, for people interested in this topic

messages.author: steel0229e
messages.content: In theory every set is either a forced win for one side or a draw. Sometimes it is easy to find the forcing lines, sometimes it is not.

messages.author: steel0229e
messages.content: I also refused to believe this for years so I completely understand you. Until this very day I think Prismata is the most balanced competitive game (maybe 2nd to Go), but I no longer have illusions.

messages.author: drevoed
messages.content: got a link to your channel?

messages.author: msven
messages.content:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=eScxdpwBLx8&index=3&list=PL9gDhaOQQIWkN79kVFwuJuNYeGjmxEOCM
Playing Your Position - Prismata Expert Strategy and Tactics Part 3
If you're interested in hanging out and asking questions while I play Prismata, check out my twitch channel: https://www.twitch.tv/msven Follow me on Twitter...

messages.author: msven
messages.content: Prismata's balance is great, these kinds of player favouring sets don't come up that often and are usually a result of a few specific units needing stat changes

messages.author: msven
messages.content: but i also acknowledge that there will always be an occasional autoloss, where I've spent like an hour or more in analyze mode and couldn't find a way to win

messages.author: msven
messages.content: it's just something you have to accept when both players are in the top 20

messages.author: Deleted User
messages.content: So you're telling me this game does have randomness 🤔

messages.author: msven
messages.content: compared to something like hearthstone, there's basically none at all

messages.author: unctious
messages.content: Not in terms of during gameplay, but randomized decks, no?

messages.author: steel0229e
messages.content: There are 2 kinds of randomness: going first or second, and outcome uncertainty where a certain position or decision leads to a series of very complex lines and it is almost impossible to tell which one is the best.

messages.author: msven
messages.content: outcome uncertainty can be improved with years of experience though

messages.author: Deleted User
messages.content: Outcome uncertainty technically isnt random. I'd say the two kinds of randomness are the set generation and going first or second, althought i suppose you could argue to call it one kind of randomness, which is "game initialization"

messages.author: steel0229e
messages.content: you can make better and better guesses the more experience you have

messages.author: steel0229e
messages.content: the random set is the 3rd type of randomness

[2018-04-06 11:23] kaom. (Kaom): So, what's the idea behind 1st and 2nd player? I mean, what is made to compensate the fact that the first player plays first  and how balanced is that?
 (i'm relatively new)
[2018-04-06 11:23] darkeisbein: well p2 gets an extra drone
[2018-04-06 11:23] redzonex (Redzone): p2 begins with an extra drone
[2018-04-06 11:23] darkeisbein: which is so good they have a slightly higher average winrate then p1
[2018-04-06 11:24] redzonex (Redzone): since you start out making two drones a turn, having an extra drone puts p2 a half-turn ahead and thus nullifies the first player advantage
[2018-04-06 11:25] redzonex (Redzone): each player has different openings that are more efficient, but p2 has two generally strong ones which account for the slight favor to p2 (48-52)
